---
title: "Reading Data"
---

<span class="time-badge">⏱ 15 minutes</span>

### Reading Data from a File

Real clinical datasets live in files, not Python objects. `pandas` can read CSV, Excel, JSON, and many other formats. In an ideal world every file would be perfectly formatted — but real-world health data exports rarely are. This section walks through reading a messy file step by step, fixing one issue at a time.

::: {.callout-note}
## nhs_readmission.csv dataset

This dataset is entirely synthetic, generated purely as a teaching example.

- Simulated distributions based on typical NHS inpatient population parameters (e.g. age ~Normal(62, 16), length of stay ~Exponential(4.5), HbA1c ~Normal(60, 18))
- Clinically plausible missingness patterns (HbA1c missing in ~16% of patients, eGFR in ~9%)
- A logistic outcome model where readmission probability depends on age, Charlson index, length of stay, prior ED attendances, eGFR, and IMD decile
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
### Column descriptions

| Column | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `patient_id` | Anonymous patient identifier |
| `age` | Age in years at admission |
| `sex` | M / F |
| `imd_decile` | Index of Multiple Deprivation (1 = most deprived, 10 = least) |
| `primary_diagnosis` | ICD-10 code for primary diagnosis |
| `charlson_index` | Charlson Comorbidity Index (0–37) |
| `hba1c` | Most recent HbA1c (mmol/mol) — missing if not tested |
| `egfr` | Most recent eGFR (mL/min/1.73m²) — missing if not tested |
| `bmi` | Body Mass Index (kg/m²) |
| `los_days` | Length of stay in days |
| `ed_prior_year` | ED attendances in previous 12 months |
| `discharge_ward` | Ward the patient was discharged from |
| `readmitted_30d` | Readmitted within 30 days: Yes / No |

:::

---

### What does the raw file look like?

Before writing any code, it helps to open the file in a text editor and look at it directly:

```
NHS Readmission Dataset - Synthetic Teaching Data
Generated for training purposes only. Not for clinical use.
Missing values encoded as: . N/A NULL UNKNOWN

patient_id,age,sex,imd_decile,primary_diagnosis,charlson_index,hba1c,egfr,bmi,los_days,ed_prior_year,discharge_ward,readmitted_30d
1001,69,M,8,E11,2,67.6,29.4,36.4,5,1,Endocrine,No
1002,59,M,2,I10,4,67.5,42.8,37.8,4,2,General Medicine,No
...
9998,150,M,4,E11,3,66.1,52.0,27.9,5,1,Endocrine,No
9999,45,F,6,K57,1,NULL,71.2,5.1,2,0,General Medicine,No
```


### Step 1 — A first attempt

```{python}
import pandas as pd

url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pauerola/nhs-data-science/refs/heads/main/data/nhs_readmission.csv"

df = pd.read_csv(url)
df.head()
```

This raises an error — pandas tries to use the first header line as column names and fails to parse the rest.

---

### Step 2 — Skip the header rows

We use `skiprows=3` to ignore the first 3 lines before looking for column names:

```{python}
df = pd.read_csv(
    url,
    skiprows=3,  # Add this
)
df.head()
```

Now we have proper column names and a correctly structured `DataFrame`.


### Step 3 — Set the row index

By default pandas assigns integer row numbers (0, 1, 2, …). Since `patient_id` uniquely identifies each row, it makes a better index:

```{python}
df = pd.read_csv(
    url,
    skiprows=3,
    index_col="patient_id",  # Add this
)
df.head()
```

Now each row is labelled by patient ID rather than an arbitrary number.


### Step 4 — Handle missing values

We noticed that the file uses `.`, `N/A`, `NULL`, and `UNKNOWN` as missing markers — `pandas` won't recognise these automatically. We fix this with `na_values`:

```{python}
df = pd.read_csv(
    url,
    skiprows=3,
    index_col="patient_id",
    na_values=[".", "N/A", "NULL", "UNKNOWN"],  # Add this
)

print("Missing values per column:")
print(df.isnull().sum())
```

::: {.callout-note}
### Why is data missing?

Missing `hba1c` (≈16%) likely means the test was not ordered — common in surgical or trauma admissions. Missing `egfr` (≈9%) may mean no recent blood tests. In machine learning, "missing" can itself be an informative signal. Always think about **why** data is absent before deciding how to handle it.
:::


### Step 5 — Explore the loaded dataset

```{python}
df.describe().round(1)
```

```{python}
print(df["readmitted_30d"].value_counts())
print(df["readmitted_30d"].value_counts(normalize=True).round(2))
```

We have a **class imbalance**: roughly 77% not readmitted, 23% readmitted — something to account for when fitting models.




### Querying Data

#### `value_counts()` — Frequency tables

```{python}
print(df["primary_diagnosis"].value_counts().head(10))
```

#### `groupby()` — Aggregation by group

`groupby()` splits the dataset into groups and applies a function to each — the pandas equivalent of a PivotTable.

```{python}
df.groupby("readmitted_30d")[["age", "charlson_index", "los_days"]].agg(["mean", "median"]).round(1)
```

Readmitted patients are on average 8 years older, have higher comorbidity burden, and stay nearly twice as long.


::: {.callout-tip}
### Exercise

1. How many unique values are in the `primary_diagnosis` column?  
   (*Hint*: `df["column"].nunique()`)
2. What is the mean HbA1c for readmitted vs non-readmitted patients?  
   (*Hint*: `df.groupby("readmitted_30d")["hba1c"].mean()`)
3. Which IMD decile has the highest readmission rate?  
   (*Hint*: `df.groupby("imd_decile")["readmitted_30d"].apply(lambda x: (x=="Yes").mean())`)
:::

::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"}
### Answer

```{python}
# 1.
print(df["primary_diagnosis"].nunique())

# 2.
print(df.groupby("readmitted_30d")["hba1c"].mean().round(1))

# 3.
readmit_by_imd = df.groupby("imd_decile")["readmitted_30d"].apply(
    lambda x: (x == "Yes").mean()
)
print(readmit_by_imd.sort_values(ascending=False))
```
:::
